items.q: What is the central principle of situation ethics?
items.correct: The only intrinsically good thing is agape love; rules can be broken if doing so produces the most loving outcome in the situation.
items.misconception: You should do whatever feels right in the moment.
items.random_wrong: Situation ethics says rules should always be followed.
items.why: Situation ethics is principled (love), not just intuition or relativism. Students collapse it into 'whatever feels right' — Fletcher's actual theory is more disciplined than that.

items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What does Kant's categorical imperative require?
items.correct: Acting only on maxims you could will to be universal laws, and treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
items.misconception: Treat others how you would like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Do whatever your conscience tells you to do.
items.why: The Golden Rule depends on your personal preferences (Kant explicitly rejected it). The categorical imperative tests universalisability — whether the maxim can coherently be a law for everyone.

items.q: Is situation ethics just utilitarianism with the word 'love' swapped in?
items.correct: Not quite — both weigh outcomes, and Fletcher even spoke of an 'agapeic calculus', but the criterion is agape (selfless neighbour-love rooted in Christian commitment), not aggregate pleasure or preference.
items.misconception: Situation ethics is identical to utilitarianism because both calculate consequences.
items.why: The structural similarity is a fair discussion point, but Fletcher's maximand and warrant differ: love posited by faith, persons before principles. Treating them as identical erases the theory's Christian core.

items.q: Is Fletcher's situation ethics a form of antinomianism?
items.correct: No — Fletcher explicitly rejects both legalism and antinomianism as extremes; his 'third way' enters every situation armed with the community's rules, prepared to set them aside only if love demands.
items.misconception: Situation ethics is antinomian: it throws away all rules and enters each situation with nothing.
items.random_wrong: Situation ethics is legalistic: rules must never be broken.
items.why: The three approaches (legalism, antinomianism, situationism) open Fletcher's book precisely so he can position himself between the extremes. Calling him antinomian repeats the error he pre-empted.

items.q: In what year was Situation Ethics published?
items.why: It's 1966. “1955” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which Greek word names the love that is Fletcher's sole moral absolute?
items.why: It's Agape. “Eros” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many working principles does Fletcher identify?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
